reasoning: The informal statement asks to show that an uncountable subset E ⊂ ℝ has a *condensation point* — a point p such that every neighborhood of p meets E in an UNCOUNTABLE set (the corrected formalization: ∀ U ∈ 𝓝 p, ¬ Set.Countable (U ∩ E)). The original formalization uses `ClusterPt p (𝓟 E)`, which by Mathlib's `clusterPt_principal_iff` unfolds to ∀ U ∈ 𝓝 p, (U ∩ E).Nonempty — merely that p is a limit/cluster point of E (every neighborhood meets E non-emptily). Non-emptiness is strictly weaker than uncountability, so the original formalization is a weaker statement than the informal claim (every condensation point is a cluster point, but not vice versa). The original weaker statement is still provably true (an uncountable subset of ℝ certainly has a cluster point, since a set with no cluster points would be closed discrete, hence countable in the Lindelöf space ℝ). The error is a misunderstanding of what "condenses" means in the informal statement — the author equated "condensation point" with the Mathlib notion `ClusterPt`, missing the uncountability requirement that distinguishes condensation from mere accumulation.
